data.title: Waters Corporation: Instrument Replacement Cycle Momentum, But Valuation Is A Concern
data.description:
Introduction

Waters Corporation (NYSE:WAT) reported their Q4’24 and FY’24 results on February 12 and the results came in better than expected. Over the last few quarters, the company has been coming out of a downturn in instruments that have been longer and deeper than prior cycles. In the latest results, we’ve seen the recovery is starting to pick up with strength led by the Academics and Government segment and pharma showing continued momentum. In this piece, I’ll analyze the most recent quarterly results and provide my thoughts on the company’s near-term and long-term outlook, as well as how I’m thinking about valuation today.

Gross margin hiccup, but FX to blame

Waters Corp closed out 2024 with a strong quarter that beat on both the top and bottom line. During the quarter, the company delivered revenue growth of 6.4% compared to last year with sales clocking in at $872.7 million, beating estimates by $15.9 million. That said, coming into the quarter, the company had a tough setup going in as investors likely expected a strong Q4 given strong instrumentation growth from rest of peers and for management to guide FY’25 conservatively at mid-single digit growth. And even though management delivered on these expectations, the gross margins seemed to have weighed on the stock-post quarter.

In terms of margins, gross margins came in at 60.0% compared to last year’s 61.2% which is obviously not something you want to see for a business that supposedly is high margin with pricing power. On the company’s earnings call, management discussed how foreign exchange headwinds were about 170bps, implying close to 100% drop-through, which is well above what we have seen in the past and what they have discussed prior. Given the strength of the U.S. dollar in recent months, the typical offsets/hedges were probably not enough to offset the velocity in the rate changes that the company saw in the quarter and likely explains some of the elevated impact.

As for the other expense line items, SG&A as a percentage of revenues decreased, as did R&D and net interest expense. Notwithstanding the gross margin impact from FX then, I’d say that this was a good quarter in my view, especially with EPS of $4.10 up 13% year over year and beating Wall Street estimates by 7 cents in spite of it all.

Pacing of instrument replacement cycle is key

As for what drove results this quarter, in Q4’24, Waters Corp characterized the budget flush as “typical” (meaning stronger than the last 2 years but trending toward a normalized level) and noted it saw a high-teens ramp sequentially in the quarter, above expectations of mid-teens growth, driven by the large pharma instrument replacement cycle beginning and a budget flush benefit in Europe, which grew 11% overall including 20% in academic in the region. On the earnings call management pointed out that historically, when coming out of a trough in instrument replacement, Waters Corp has usually seen 2-3% outperformance long-term instrument growth average of 5%.

For the growth drivers of Waters Corp’s business, revenues this quarter have been driven by 10% organic growth in Pharma, a 2% increase in Industrial, and 16% growth in Academia and Government driven by the strength in markets like Asia and Europe. One of the things that stood out to me on the earnings call was that the company emphasized that spending by large pharma, CDMOs, and generics customers is continuing to recover.

By product group, instrument revenues grew 8% organically while recurring revenues like chemistry and service revenues 9% organically, split between 7% growth in chemistry and 9% growth in service. In terms of specific products, the Alliance IS represented approximately 20% of the company’s HPLC revenues in Q4’24 as its adoption increased throughout the year. In MS, sales of the Xevo TQ Absolute system grew by 40% year over year, contributing around half of tandem quad sales. While these are smaller parts of Waters Corp’s business, they do highlight some of the growth drivers within their portfolio.

Meanwhile, in the chemistry consumables segment, Max Premier columns sales increased by 30% in the quarter, with 40% growth for the year. By geography, Americas grew 6% organically with 11% organic growth in Europe, and 9% growth in Asia. This shows that growth isn’t really limited to one region, and that demand across all markets is generally healthy.

Outlook

Looking ahead, Waters Corp is focused on executing in core business lines as well as high-growth areas such as GLP-1 testing, PFAS testing, and the generics market in India which is expected to add 70bps to 100bps. In my view, with India growing 34% driven by the expanding generics market, I expect continued market leadership by Waters Corp. Management has noted that they see significant opportunities in the generics market with some $240 billion of revenues expected to go off-patent in the next 5-7 years. With India currently supplying 50% of the global generics market, I think there’s a lot of opportunities for continued growth in the medium term.

For 2025, Waters Corp anticipates continued strong momentum, with growth projected between 4.5% and 7%. Despite the strengthening U.S. dollar, which is expected to create foreign exchange headwinds, the company plans to counterbalance these impacts through robust operational performance sustaining its margin expansion trends into 2025. This translates to a full-year earnings per fully diluted share guidance of $12.70 to $13 which implies 7% to 10% growth compared to last year.

In my view, the guidance seems achievable. Management is expecting recurring revenue growth of 6-7%, with instrument growth around 4.5%. To me, this looks realistic given that recurring revenue for WAT has consistently grown in this range, even through different macroeconomic cycles so it’s reassuring that they’re confident in this trend continuing.

As for instruments, the company expects steady growth across key areas like mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography, and thermal analysis. From what I can tell, the optimism about 2025 seems justified, especially with the replacement cycle in large pharma kicking off and new products like Alliance in HPLC and Xevo TQ in mass spectrometry gaining traction. Plus, there are a few emerging growth drivers, like PFAS and generics in India, that could add some extra momentum.

One thing that stood out to me was that pricing is also expected to continue supporting growth, which, I think, makes sense given how consistently it’s contributed in recent years. When we look at the breakdown by end-market, pharma is projected to grow around 7%, which is realistic considering the sector’s stability, with Industrial growing at a slower pace around 5%. The academic/government sector is a bit more uncertain, especially with a dip expected (guide from management) in Q1’25, but I think management’s cautious approach here makes sense given the potential for shifts in funding.

Risks and valuation

In terms of the risks to the investment thesis, I think the biggest risk is a lack of meaningful opportunities to deploy cash. Multiples in the space are elevated and buybacks make less sense with valuations close to all-time highs. In my view, this risk supersedes the concern around the pace and sustainability of the analytical instrument market's recovery. Long-term, I’m pretty sure this can recover, and management seems optimistic about potential instrument replacement cycle driving growth but with a slow rebound in customer capex spending. Still, the company is still reliant on the timing of this cycle and much of the risk comes down to the price investors are paying versus the value they are getting.

On valuation, I find it hard to justify the current price given the growth ahead. When looking at sell side estimates for the stock, most analysts see high-single digit to low-double digit earnings growth going forward, mostly driven by multiple expansion and share buybacks as the top line grows around 5% per year.

However, when we consider that investors today are paying 29.0x earnings or 21.0x EV/EBITDA for a medium growth company, I think shares are likely fairly priced at best (source: Bloomberg). Indeed, Waters Corp trades at premiums of 20% and 17%, respectively, relative to the company’s long-term five-year average. It also trades at a premium to the peer group average that trades at 24.2x P/E and 18.0x EV/EBITDA.

All in all, I think Waters Corp’s latest results highlight a solid recovery driven by growth in key sectors like academia, government, and pharma. Despite a slight dip in gross margins due to foreign exchange headwinds, the company delivered strong performance with healthy growth in both instrument and recurring revenue categories. Looking ahead, I’m optimistic about the company’s exposure to emerging markets such as India and in high-growth product areas like GLP-1 and PFAS testing. However, I do find the company’s current valuation somewhat challenging, as it appears to be priced at a premium compared to its historical averages and peers. So while I remain confident in the company’s long-term growth potential, I think it’s important for investors to balance this with the price they’re paying today. For now, I rate the stock as a ‘hold’.

data.title: Rates Spark: Eurozone PMIs Will Test Positive Macro Sentiment
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Benjamin Schroeder

PMIs have less influence over the front end than before

Data surprises have turned more positive for the eurozone recently, and the round of PMIs today will be the next test. The last reading of the eurozone PMI composite index edged above the 50 breakeven level again, thanks to an uptick in manufacturing. But overall, the manufacturing industry continues to show signs of weakness, with the German index in contractionary territory for over two years now.

Markets seem in good spirits in terms of the growth outlook and would not be happy to see a turn lower in PMIs. Equities have shown an exceptionally strong performance this year, and credit spreads have tightened further. Yet, we shouldn’t forget how quickly sentiment can turn. November last year saw rates significantly below today on the back of mounting growth concerns, both in the US and the eurozone. One round of weak PMIs was enough to justify an additional rate cut by the European Central Bank in October.

Since the lows in November, the ECB has cut the policy rate by another 50bp, limiting the potential impact from PMIs on the front end of the curve. Recent hawkish comments by the ECB have pushed back against the idea of cutting below 2%. Markets have stuck to the view that the ECB will land between 1.75% and 2% for some months now, and breaking that range would need more than one bad PMI reading. And if the weakness were to be driven by Germany, the usual suspect as of late, then the foresight of elections this Sunday may overshadow a downside surprise.

Sunday's German elections are also a reality check on market's spending expectations

The expected ramping up of military spending has pushed bond yields higher, and in the case of Bunds, the relative pricing to swaps is at its cheapest levels. The uncertainty ahead of the German elections and the implications for the constitutional debt brake were another factor pressuring Bund valuations. The outcome, and in particular the performance of smaller parties, will determine whether those in favour of - or at least open to - reforms of the brake will have the necessary votes. Looming large is also a fear that the right-wing AfD could perform better than polls suggest.

Even if a majority for reforms is obtained, we have the feeling that after all the headlines also surrounding the military spending, markets might overestimate the extent of the reform and also the timelines that might still be involved - starting with the formation of the government and then moving to the actual topic itself. Remarks from the election front-runner CDU suggest that other options of finding the financing, such as savings, should be prioritised first. The initial market reaction might be to still cheapen Bunds relatively, but we see a chance that such a move could be short-lived.

Today’s events and market view

From the eurozone, we first have French and German PMIs and then the aggregate indices. The composite index for France is expected at 48, still in contractionary territory. For Germany, consensus sees a 50.8 composite reading, dragged down by a 45.5 for manufacturing. The eurozone aggregate composite index is expected to remain close to 50. UK PMIs are also expected to remain close to 50, and for the US, a relatively stronger 53.2 is pencilled in for the composite component. From the US, we will also see the University of Michigan indices and existing home sales.

Italy will open subscriptions for an 8Y BTP Volare retail bond for the last day.

data.title: Artisan High Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Discussion

Our portfolio outperformed the ICE BofA US High Yield Index during the quarter. From an asset class perspective, the most significant positive contributor was security selection in the corporate bond component. In addition, the portfolio’s allocation to bank loans was additive in a quarter where Treasury rates generally rose across the yield curve. By rating, security selection in B-rated securities was the largest contributor, although the portfolio benefited from positive attribution effects in all three major rating categories (BB, B, CCC). Across sectors, the most notable contributors included security selection in insurance and retail while security selection in consumer goods and leisure detracted from returns.

Investing Environment

In a stark reversal of Q3, Treasury yields rose significantly across the curve as investors recalibrated interest rate expectations following the US elections, hawkish Federal Reserve commentary and a continued strong economic environment. During the quarter, the 10-year Treasury yield rose 79bps and erased its entire decline from Q3— ending the year close to its peak for 2024. In addition, the yield curve continued to steepen as the 2-10 spread rose 20bps with the yield curve ending the year at its steepest point since May 2022. All told, the 10-year Treasury yield ended the year up 95bps from its low in September, creating a volatile environment for duration-sensitive assets.

Against this backdrop, credit markets generated positive returns in Q4 resulting in high-single-digit returns across bonds and loans for the year. The ICE BofA US High Yield Index gained 0.2% for the quarter to end 2024 with full-year returns of 8.2%. Spreads tightened modestly by 11bps in the quarter, combining with coupon returns to help offset duration-driven price declines. Meanwhile, the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index returned 2.3% for the quarter and 9.1% for the year, registering positive returns for each month of 2024 and providing a stable base of return in a year with high rate-driven volatility. The average price of the loan index rose over a full point during the year and coupon spreads declined 22bps on the back of continued positive technicals (low net issuance) and repricings.

Within high yield bond markets, returns in 2024 were led by the CCC segment—in particular, distressed credits. For the year, the ICE BofA US High Yield CCC & Lower Index gained 18.2%, more than doubling the return of both the BB-rated (6.3%) and B-rated (7.5%) parts of the market. This outperformance accelerated in the second half of 2024 as distressed names began to be bid up by the market amid the start of the Fed’s new easing cycle and continued strong economic data. The ICE BofA US Distressed Index gained 24.7% for the year with virtually all of its return generated in the 3rd and 4th quarters. In comparison, a universe of non-distressed CCCs maintained by BofA returned 14.5% in 2024—outperforming the full high yield index but lagging behind their distressed brethren. In time periods of “junk rallies” such as the second half of 2024, we continue to advocate an approach centered on business quality and fundamental issuer strength. Over the long term, we believe outperformance in CCCs is best generated through keen security selection that seeks to identify fundamentally mis-rated securities, which we think gives us the best chance to generate attractive returns while avoiding permanent capital impairment.

As leveraged loans continue to generate consistent coupon income with low volatility, it’s worth putting into context their attractive relative and risk-adjusted returns across both recent and longer-term periods. On a trailing 1-, 3-, 5- and 10-year basis, the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index has outperformed both the ICE BofA US High Yield Index and the ICE BofA US Broad Market Index (a reference for the investment grade bond universe). In each of those periods, the loan index’s return per unit of volatility (standard deviation) was significantly higher than its fixed rate counterparts. In addition, the low correlation of the loan index to investment grade bonds during this period provided a valuable complement to traditional asset allocations that may be more heavily dominated by fixed rate assets. It’s not often that you can find a diversifying asset with compelling absolute and risk-adjusted return potential, but investors have found these characteristics in loans over the past 10 years. Our portfolio has continued to benefit from exposure in this area as we take a highly selective, fundamentally oriented approach to the market.

One of the most notable themes in credit markets in 2024 was the robustness of capital markets activity. On a gross supply basis, high yield issuance increased more than 60% year-over-year while leveraged loans recorded their highest annual issuance ever, significantly surpassing previous peaks in 2021 and 2017. Many issuers took advantage of more favorable market pricing and positive technicals to refinance and reprice their debt, extending maturities and achieving interest cost savings. Nevertheless, as we have discussed in the past, net issuance—gross supply minus refinancings and repricings—remains low driven by an M&A market that is still depressed relative to 2021. We believe that M&A will likely rebound in 2025 driven by a potentially more pro-business administration and deregulatory environment, which could create more net supply for our market. This dynamic may also result in increased interest from higher credit quality strategic buyers to acquire leveraged companies, introducing opportunities for investors to reap benefits as discounted debt of an acquisition target could reprice to par or above.

Excluding distressed exchanges, default rates in high yield and loans have been declining since early 2023 and are well below long-term averages. The most significant increase in headline defaults in 2024 came in the form of liability management exercises, or LMEs. While the frequency of mentions of “creditor-on-creditor violence” in the financial press seems to imply that LMEs are ubiquitous, in reality they continue to represent a very small part of the market. For example, according to JP Morgan data, there were 48 companies that completed a distressed exchange in 2024; in total, there are estimated to be over 1,800 companies across leveraged credit markets. We continue to monitor this space and selectively participate across our platform. Often, the threat of an LME results in downward pressure on asset prices through secondary market selling. Our team has experience spanning the gamut from performing to distressed, enabling us to dig in deep on heavily discounted assets to potentially find value.

Portfolio Positioning

Throughout 2024, we increased our allocation to leveraged loans as we identified several attractive opportunities in single names. In particular, in the second half of the year, we purchased loans of two issuers in the primary market that were discounted at issuance. In each circumstance, the syndicate banks assigned to market the deal struggled to clear the deal near par, forcing them to offload the debt at material original issue discounts (OID) as well as requiring the company and its sponsor to offer more creditor-friendly documentation. After conducting deep due diligence—including an analysis of product profitability at the individual stock keeping unit (SKU) level—we elected to participate in the deals and allocate in size.

Insurance and leisure continue to represent our largest exposures by sector. Within insurance, we continue to favor the insurance brokerage segment of the market, which we believe offers compelling risk-adjusted yields from quality companies with high recurring revenue and low capex needs—characteristics we believe are highly valuable, particularly in an environment of tighter spreads. The leisure segment retains its focus on the cruise lines, which continue their strong operating performance. As a reminder, while we believe this exposure offers attractive risk-adjusted return potential at current levels, the cap structures for these names are deep and liquid—giving us the ability to monetize our exposure and deploy elsewhere if/when a market dislocation occurs.

Given the performance of CCCs and distressed in 2024, it’s worth reminding investors about our fundamental approach to the asset class and emphasis on business quality. While our CCC-rated exposure overall outperformed the benchmark and was additive from an allocation perspective, our CCCs lagged the CCC component of the index. A significant portion of our CCC exposure is the aforementioned insurance broker market segment, which tends to have less volatility than the broader CCC market but also trades at tighter spreads. As our process is predicated on identifying businesses that—first and foremost—are quality companies, the bulk of our CCC-rated exposure was invested in sectors like insurance brokers rather than the more distressed companies that participated in the “junk rally” in the second half of the year.

Perspective

Credit markets performed well in 2024, with elevated all-in yields and modest spread tightening helping to produce high-single-digit returns. Loans outperformed bonds during the year aided by their lack of interest rate sensitivity as well as higher coupon income. The high yield market continues to trade at tighter spread levels, but absent an exogenous shock we believe spreads will likely be rangebound in the near term. In addition, current yield levels across bonds and loans offer what we believe to be compelling risk-adjusted total return potential and a valuable complement to asset allocations.

As we look forward to 2025, there remains pockets of idiosyncratic value in credit which we seek to take advantage of in our portfolio. We continue to believe that an investment approach centered on business quality, fundamental issuer strength and high-conviction portfolio construction is of utmost importance in today’s market. As our investors know, we will never sacrifice credit discipline for the sake of chasing yields and returns.

data.title: Why The EU's New Agri-Focused Vision Matters For The Food Industry
data.description:
By Thijs Geijer

Greenhouse gas reduction targets - ambition versus reality

In its long-awaited Vision on agriculture and food, the EU states that the Commission 'expects agriculture to achieve the emission cuts in alignment with the EU's climate target for 2030', but without mentioning the 55% target explicitly. That commitment from policymakers should comfort corporates in the food industry, given that their Scope 3 targets largely rely on progress at farm level.

Still, we remain largely in the dark on exactly how the EU Commission plans to achieve this, aside from the fact that incentives, carbon removals and technological advancements should help to do the trick. And we have doubts on how realistic it is, given that the reduction in agricultural emissions currently stands at around -25% compared to 1990. With just five more years to go, projections from the European Environment Agency show that the sector will be falling short of the broader target.

Voluntary benchmarking - EU could learn from existing schemes

Rolling out mandatory on-farm benchmarking on sustainability parameters is proving to be a political no-go. Still, the Commission aims to get more farmers engaged in sustainability benchmarking by developing an 'on-farm sustainability compass' with input from various stakeholders. Some food manufacturers have already rolled out such schemes. We see a clear opportunity for these companies to share best practices with policymakers - and, in turn, provide valuable input for the Commission's 'Compass'.

Generational renewal - farmers of the future

The fates of farmers and food processors are often intertwined. A lack of perspective for farmers could reduce supply and lead to higher prices and excess capacity in production, which is most worrying for companies that depend on a 'local' farm base (like meat, dairy and sugar processors). So if the Commission is taking the lead on a Generational Renewal Strategy to slow down the drop in the number of farmers and the pressure on farm land, that's also positive news for food companies and suppliers of farm inputs.

Don't expect any bold or swift action on livestock

It's not surprising that the Commission is promising a long-term vision for the livestock sector, given its economic weight; livestock accounts for a third of EU food exports. However, it also remains the most carbon-intensive part of the agri-food value chain. The direction the Commission takes on this will be crucial, particularly for EU meat and dairy processors. The Vision mentions technological advancements as a solution, including feeding strategies. In our view, that's a signal that policymakers will continue to be supportive of feed additives and other feed-based solutions. But for more particular recommendations, we have to await the results from a 'livestock work stream' that still needs to be set up.

Emissions Trading System - agriculture not in scope

You can search long and hard for it, but you won't find it in the text. We think it's safe to say this won't be introduced in the foreseeable future.

Trade: Directorate-General for Agri's stance on trade creates challenges and opportunities

The Vision takes a tougher stance on the residue levels of the most hazardous pesticides on imported food products like fruits and vegetables, and makes it clear that high EU standards on animal welfare should also apply to imported meat, dairy and eggs. This is supportive for EU farmers as it raises the bar for their competitors. For some EU importers of fruits and vegetables, like citrus fruit, it will require more effort to make sure their suppliers comply.

At the same time, the drive for more reciprocity when it comes to animal welfare standards makes it more complicated to strike trade deals with large meat exporters such as Australia. The Vision also promises a 'comprehensive protein strategy', which would primarily focus on animal feed and reducing dependencies on imports (mainly from South America). This is at odds with the recently agreed EU Mercosur trade agreement and could resurface during the ratification process of the trade agreement, which is expected in the second half of 2025.

EU funding: Agriculture faces fierce competition for EU funds

Food security, energy security, safety - they're all important. But when you take into account what the Vision says about public support for agriculture (better targeted) and the broader debate on the next EU budget, it seems quite probable that the share of agriculture in the budget will decrease - especially because energy and defence security are critical issues for the EU. The fact that there is no mention of an Agri-Food Just Transition Fund in the Vision is a clear signal that calls by stakeholders for more public funding are not being answered. As a result, we expect more calls from policymakers to the private sector to step in when it comes to financing.

Steering demand: EU delegates responsibility to national governments

As we've indicated previously, there is very little appetite to influence or steer consumer demand at the EU level. Scientists consider a partial shift away from animal-based to plant-based food an effective strategy to make the food system more sustainable - but plant-based food isn't featured in the Vision in any meaningful way. Part of the argumentation is that the main competence lies not in Brussels but with national governments. Since many national governments aren't keen on demand-side policies either, we expect that these tools will largely remain in the toolbox.

data.title: Core Bonds: Unlocking Value In A Volatile Market
data.description:
Transcript

Mohit Mittal: When we think about broad active risks, we try to focus on three key pillars of those active risks.

First is around structural inefficiencies in fixed income markets. Our view is that in fixed income markets, because of prevalence of balance sheet constraints, regulatory constraints, liquidity constraints, dependence upon rating agencies as well as presence of non-economic buyers like passive investors, creates a lot of structural inefficiencies that we as active managers can take advantage of.

Next pillar for us is around thematic active views. This is where all of our active research at the top down from macro point of view, policy point of view, as well as fundamental credit research comes into play.

And then finally, the third pillar for us is around opportunistic investing. This is where we just have to be a little bit patient. We have to wait for the market to come to us, and you don't see as many dislocations at any time. But generally, you tend to see overshoots during periods introduced by geopolitics or liquidity events.

There are plenty of areas that we see opportunities for active adjustment in the portfolio to take advantage of many of the dislocations.

In the corporate credit space, what we have seen is that liquidity premium has come down. What we have done in the portfolio is reduce our allocation to credit, replace it with higher quality spread products like agency mortgages, like structured products, and that limits the downside in the portfolio during periods of volatility.

More recently what we have seen is a pretty significant repricing in the US interest rates. Following that repricing, we have meaningfully increased the duration risk in the portfolio. We have gone from about a half a year underweight six months ago to now almost 0.75 years overweight on the duration space.

Global relative value continues to play an important role. We like sovereign bonds in Canada, in UK, and in Australia.

Inflation as well as deficits continue to get reflected through tips in the portfolio. One of our macro themes is uncertainty is certain, and this particular environment, uncertainty levels will be high. I think there will be volatility, there will be dislocations. What we have done is increase the dry powder in the portfolio to take advantage of those opportunities.

I think the net of it is that the reason for why we think fixed income is so attractive, stems from higher yield, stems from the diversification benefit, and stems from the reason that the relative value versus cash as well as equities looks quite attractive.

data.title: VSE Corporation: Selling The Fleet Business On The Cheap
data.description:
In November of last year, I believed that there were many moving parts in the case of VSE Corporation (NASDAQ:VSEC). This came after shares doubled so far in 2024 amidst elevated dealmaking activities.

The many developments going on in combination with higher expectations made me unease, and I was awaiting clarity on the 2025 outlook before considering an investment.

An Aftermarket Distribution And MRO Play For Aerospace And Fleet Customers

The header above is basically what VSE is doing and is about. The company aims to improve productivity and lifespan of both high-value and business-critical assets.

Back in 2023, the company generated some $860 million in sales, generated and reported across two divisions. The largest of these was a $544 million aviation segment in which the company offers repair and MRO services to commercial, business, general aviation and rotorcraft operators.

The company furthermore operates a $316 million fleet management business in which it distributes parts, engineering solutions and supply chain solutions for medium and heavy-duty markets and solutions.

The company saw a very successful 2023, as full year revenues were up 29% to $860 million, with adjusted EBITDA up as much as 45% to $114 million, and adjusted earnings up a similar 45% to $3.31 per share. These solid results were aided by a strategic reset in the years before, laying down the groundwork for organic growth to return, complemented by M&A efforts.

A $70 stock early in 2024 looked to be valued at a more than reasonable price, also given that net debt of $422 million yielded a 3.7 times leverage ratio, in part due to M&A actions undertaken in recent years.

2024 - Moving Ahead

Amidst many trends early in 2024, the company guided for aviation revenues to rise by some 26%, with fleet sales seen up 15%. There were some moving targets, as the company announced a $120 million acquisition of Turbine Controls, while the company divested the Federal & Defense segment in transactions valued at $44 million.

After a strong 28% increase in first quarter sales, and 30% increase in second quarter sales, the company announced a $200 million deal to acquire Kellstrom Aerospace in the fall. The deal would add about $175 million in sales as 15% EBITDA margins make the deal look quite compelling, driven by an anticipated $4 million in annual synergies.

Shares jumped from $90 to $105 upon the deal announcement, adding $300 million to the valuation of the firm. This cannot entirely be attributed to the deal itself, as the company furthermore announced solid preliminary third quarter results alongside the deal announcement. As it turned out, third quarter sales were reported up another 18%, although that earnings were down a bit following issues with USPS, resulting in lower activity levels within the fleet segment.

Pro forma I pegged the share count at 20.5 million shares, granting the company a $2.4 billion equity valuation at $117 per share, back in November. This included a $442 million net debt load as of the third quarter, set to remain pretty stable with much of the Kellstrom deal paid for in shares.

A resulting $2.8 billion valuation looked reasonable, with revenues trending at $1.3 billion, valued at just over 2 times. However, earnings were coming in just close around $3 per share, driven by issues in the fleet business, pushing up expectations a long way. If the fleet margins could revert, I could see earnings power closer to $4 per share, as that would reduce leverage to about 3 times.

Trading at $117, it felt as if the biggest opportunity was gone, as expectations has risen a bit too much, too fast.

A Rethink

Between November and today, shares of VSE Corporation have gradually fallen to current levels around the $100 mark, and that even comes after shares recovered from a low around $90 per share in January.

The big news came in the middle of February, as VSE announced the sale of its Fleet Business to One Equity Partners in a deal valued at $230 million. The transaction structure calls for a cash component of just $140 million, a $25 million seller note and $65 million contingent earn out consideration.

With the company valued around $2.5 billion at around the $100 mark, the headline deal tag (including earn-outs) comes in at less than 10% of the total value of the firm. This feels light, given that the unit, even though it has real issues, it was responsible for no less than 36% of total sales in 2023!

Through the first nine months of 2024, fleet segment revenues fell 5% to $222 million, with segment earnings cut in half to $11 million. After corporate cost overhead, that suggests more or less break-even results, yet the price fetched feels very soft.

What Now?

While shares have lost about 15% from the November levels, which naturally increases appeal, I am cautious as I am not impressed with the sale of the fleet business, at least not the price fetched for the assets, nor the way the deal is structured.

Given all this, I reiterate my cautious stance, as the latest news is not convincing for me to alter that neutral and cautious stance on the shares yet, if anything on the contrary.

data.title: CTA: I'm Upping My Exposure To Managed Futures (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I first started covering the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:CTA) back in December, when I recommended it as a hedge against an uncertain S&P 500. CTA is a managed futures fund, meaning that it trades derivatives contracts across a variety of assets, including equities, bonds, currencies, and commodities with the goal of producing positive absolute returns in all market conditions. The reason one may include it as a hedge to an equities or bond portfolio is because it can provide positive returns in years that are bad for equities or bonds in particular, and provide an expectation of consistent positive returns. This doesn't always play out, but that's the theory behind it.

Managed futures funds have become very popular in the past few years, as savvy ETF managers have become eager to bring these kinds of "hedge fund like" tools to retail traders. What the fund is doing is actually run by a different kind of investment company than a hedge fund, called "commodities trading advisers," which is where we get the ticker CTA for Simplify's fund.

Since my first recommendation in December, it's done very well, not just as a hedge, but even as markets have reached higher and higher. Here is its performance since inception against the market, bonds, gold, and a trend-following fund (the passive version of managed futures), KMLM.

CTA has absolutely smoked most of these assets, which is impressive considering these managed futures funds are often touted as funds that should only really shine in down or sideways markets. The folks at Simplify, or more specifically the sub-adviser to the fund, Altis Partners, have really proven their chops at identifying trends and taking strategic long/short positions to capture them regardless of the market conditions.

To this end, I have also included this fund in my multi-asset portfolio, which I currently have a significant portion of my personal portfolio in.

CTA Holdings

The fund repositions itself across its various markets constantly, meaning that the fund could take new positions at will, and does on a day-to-day basis. This turnover, and the associated black box that it takes place in, can be a red flag for some investors. I do not mind active management, and believe that it can lead to outperformance, but there are folks that disagree with me.

While we can look at CTA's holdings, it's nearly impossible to speculate on why they are holding any given position. This is due to the decision-making of the management not being public knowledge, and the nature of futures making it unclear if any positions are linked to each other as pairs, or how long they intend to hold on to any given position.

This is something that investors need to be at peace with before considering an investment into a managed futures ETF, especially actively managed ones like CTA and the iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF (DBMF).

The actual positions take several screenshots to post because they are spread across an absolute ton of different futures contracts, and I don't want to make them so small that they're impossible to read in one screenshot.

If you're interested in going through the holdings, just know that they may have already changed by the time this article is published, as they can change daily and without any notice. You can find current ones updated daily on Simplify's website.

To help folks understand these holdings a bit more clearly, Simplify has also listed out their exposures to given assets. Those holdings translate to these weights, essentially, for each instrument. I am very glad that they provide this view of the fund, as it gives a lot of insight into the fund's real weightings toward each instrument, and how much they contribute to the fund's volatility.

Note that "total" is in the middle of the table, since the net weight of the fund is usually flat to slightly negative. It's currently at (1.49%).

The fund itself is very heavy on commodities and short treasuries. While we can't know exactly what the fund manager is thinking, we can get a sense of it through this view of the fund's holdings.

Also note the heavy long exposure to gold, coffee, and natural gas, which may explain some recent gains made by the fund as these commodities have done very well over the last few months. CTA is also considerably short the 5yr and 2yr UST, indicating that the managers believe that yields may continue to rise.

On a personal note, I also believe that there may be more pain in store for the 2yr and 5yr bonds. If you want to read more commentary about it, I discuss it in my report on the January CPI numbers.

The recent jobs report also caused yields to rise, as the bond market seems to be re-adjusting to the reality of returning inflation and low unemployment. I also wrote about that this month.

It seems to me that CTA is positioned to be long almost all commodities, as we see inflation push prices up, leading to the Fed pushing rates up (and treasuries down), cause the price of commodities across the board to rise.

That's about as much as I can comment on their positioning, as the rest of it is in commodities that I am not familiar enough with to comment on why they may be a good position or not. Apologies to those who wanted more commentary on why they have over a 10% allocation to corn. That's just part of the black box that Altis Partners operates within.

CTA's 10% Yield

The main attraction for a lot of folks when it comes to these funds, outside the diversification reason, is that they can have high yields. Simplify has CTA's distribution rate at 10.62%, as of the end of last year.

Simplify has been accused of "running experiments with their live funds," by commenters on my articles in the past, and I understand why. The dividends were all over the place in the past.

Looking at the most recent distributions, and we see a very odd quarterly schedule, with the only actual payout so far being the December one. The rest are listed as $0.00 paid, but are still counted as distributions.

I would expect CTA to only pay out annually, moving forward. Beware that this yield is not a promise, and may not stay this high or happen at all in the future.

Suitability

CTA's performance, and its manager's keen eye for spotting trends and taking advantage of them, have given me more trust for them. Currently, CTA occupies a 10% allocation in my multi-asset portfolio. I am considering raising that allocation to 15%.

Today, I am raising my recommendation to investors. Aggressive investors could consider up to a 20% allocation to CTA as a diversifying element to an equities, income, or hybrid portfolio. While income is a weak spot for CTA since it doesn't seem to have consistency behind it, and it may only pay annually, it still carries a trailing distribution rate of 10%, making it an effective income instrument in the past.

In the past, I have recommended pairing CTA with another manager, to diversify key-person risk, e.g. the risk that the manager of the fund makes the wrong move. I still recommend pairing CTA with DBMF.

Moderate investors could consider up to a 10% allocation to CTA, or a 15% total allocation to managed futures, with a split between CTA and DBMF.

Conservative investors could consider up to a 5% allocation to managed futures, and should consider KMLM instead of CTA or DBMF to avoid active management risk altogether.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am bullish on the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF because of the past performance of active manager Altis Partners, the sub-adviser to CTA that picks and chooses its trades. They successfully have traded up along the trends of several big commodities swings in the last few months, including gold, natural gas, and coffee. They are also trading short treasuries, which, I believe, is strategic for our current economic conditions. The fund has successfully served as an example of what active managers worth their salt can accomplish. I expect this kind of insight and performance to continue from them moving forward, regardless of equity market conditions.

Because of the management and their black box strategy being so integral to returns, I am recommending that investors pair CTA up with competing managed futures ETFs, and particularly the iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF.

I am considering increasing my exposure to managed futures as a whole, and particularly to CTA, when I update my personal portfolio in the coming weeks as we head toward the end of Q1.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Dutch Bros: Brewing A Legacy - 1,000 Stores And Counting
data.description:
Hot Coffee, Hotter Results

Who needs sugar when Dutch Bros’ (NYSE:BROS) earnings are this sweet?

The coffee chain that now spans the United States from coast to coast reported another stronger-than-expected quarter, beating both forecasts for top-line sales by $24 million and EPS by $0.05. And what’s more, Dutch Bros just hit its 1,000-location milestone nationwide!

But let's take it one step at a time. We have a lot to talk about today, but don't rush! Grab a coffee and enjoy!

First things first. We saw another double-digit growth in top-line sales. They were approximately $342.8 million during Q4, growing approximately 34.9% year-over-year. This was driven by the addition of 151 new locations, as well as a tailwind of same-store sales growing 6.9% year-over-year.

Breaking down a bit of Dutch Bros same-store sales for this quarter, we can see that there was a 2.3% year-over-year increase in transactions and an average check per guest growth of 4.6% year-over-year. Both accelerated from last quarter, showing clear sequential improvements.

Surely this doesn’t just happen, does it? To achieve these sequential improvements, Dutch Bros has relied on its mobile ordering (remember when it was all a test in Arizona?), marketing efforts and maturation in certain markets like Tennessee, New Mexico and Oklahoma, as well as late-penetration markets like Florida.

Of course, a quarter of fewer discounts and higher prices also created tailwinds for the average check per guest.

Now take a look at how this played out when we look at the bigger picture. Here I've compiled annual sales, same-store sales and asset turnover data for Dutch Bros:

Note that from the moment Dutch Bros decided to grow almost exclusively the company-owned units segment, the asset turnover began to transition as the mix of corporate properties increased within the company's portfolio. The reason is clear as day, isn't it? Precisely to maintain cohesion in the value proposition and training of Broistas as they advance state-by-state.

These company-operated stores saw higher same-store sales during FY 2024. They grew 9.5% year-over-year during Q4 FY 2024, as well as saw traffic grow 5.2% year-over-year. Simply incredible!

To keep the pipeline hot as a freshly brewed coffee, Dutch Bros is aiming to see double-digit year-over-year growth in its locations. During FY 2024, they saw their location count grow 18% year-over-year, adding 151 locations. For FY 2025, they are set to add around 160 new stores, aiming to reach 1,142 locations by the end of the year. That’s 16.3% year-over-year growth, a slowdown from last year, but still stronger than almost every other restaurant/coffee chain out there.

To assess Dutch Bros’ sustainable growth and competitive advantage with the more economical boxes, we need to look inside them. In fact, in my last article I only briefly touched on this issue. Let’s delve a little deeper now.

We know that each company-operated location has the following characteristics:

  1. Construction costs around $1.8 million. Of course, Dutch Bros has plans to reduce this drastically by FY 2025 and facilitate penetration into markets with smaller DMAs, generating a more interesting ROI in medium-low volume situations. We will look at this possibility later;
  2. AUVs of approximately $2 million; and
  3. Restaurant-level margins of 29.7%. Note that they have grown significantly compared to what they were during FY 2023 (28.2%).

I want you to see for yourself how economical these boxes are:

  • Assuming an average cost of capital of 7.5% and a perpetual growth rate of 2.5%, the PV of a Dutch Bros location amounts to $10,293,483.04 (growing 5.3% year-over-year from the improvement in restaurant-level margins) and an NPV of $8,493,483.04;
  • It has a payback period and discounted payback of 4 years;
  • The Benefit-Cost Ratio is 5.72 (in FY 2023 it was 5.43). For those who don't know, this is the ratio between the sum of the projected cash flows for a location discounted to present value and the construction cost. This means that the sum of these discounted cash flows is 5.72x the costs;
  • The IRR of the construction project is 31.7%. Note that during FY 2023 it was 30.1%.

These are great numbers, but they may not be suitable for locations with weak DMAs. Well, Dutch Bros could do the same as Portillo's (PTLO) and launch leaner locations to increase penetration, which is already quite attractive. One of the keys is to reduce the payback period. To do this, Dutch Bros needs to focus on reducing its construction costs and preserving its margins.

Let's assume that Dutch Bros manages to reduce construction costs by at least 20% for FY 2025. What would be the impact on unit-level economics metrics? Here's the thread:

  • Considering construction costs of $1.44 million, we achieve a three-year payback period, as well as a discounted payback period of the same length;
  • A Cost-Benefit Ratio of 7.15, increasing at least 25% compared to what Dutch currently delivers;
  • An impressive 40% IRR.

An economic analysis doesn't mean much when we look at the industry, does it? Below I compared the data from Dutch Bros' unit projects and compared it with other restaurant chains:

Note that despite being in the top quartile when it comes to unit savings, Dutch Bros has room to improve when compared to trendy fast-casuals like Wingstop (WING) and Sweetgreen (SG), which can deliver payback periods of less than three years! It is indeed possible to achieve this. All it would take is a double-digit reduction in construction costs.

Renewed Real Estate Strategy: What Does This Mean in Practice?

But it appears that there are other plans in place alongside the prototypical developments. Dutch Bros has just entered FY 2025 with a revamped real estate strategy.

What does this mean in practice?

The first difference is the lease models. For most of its history, Dutch Bros used Ground Leases. These construction models are relatively more expensive and complicated, as Dutch only leased the land and took on the responsibility of setting up and maintaining the property. This made the average construction cost for a drive-thru location $1.9 million.

Under the new strategy, Dutch Bros has adopted Build-to-Suit Leases, where the developer builds the property to the tenant’s specifications. This improves both site selection and de-risks construction and development. This has saved the company at least $100,000, which is now working with construction costs of $1.8 million per location.

The second difference is that they have completely abandoned the Rapid Densification Strategy, as we saw in Texas. They opened about two hundred locations there in less than three years! This has led to some problems related to cannibalization, as we have seen in other concepts, especially FSRs like Kura Sushi (KRUS) in the same state.

To get around this, the drive-thru coffee shop chain will not have as its main objective rapid penetration, but rather to strengthen the brand image, investing in advertising to generate brand awareness before going all out with the openings. This is already underway in its recent expansion in Florida!

But the most interesting thing of all is that Dutch recently announced that this new strategy has increased store-level productivity. Just look at the traffic growth and you will see that the overlap has decreased a lot.

Mobile Ordering, Innovation, and Food: How Dutch Bros Plans to Keep Driving Traffic

It is worth remembering that Dutch Bros maintains its transaction generation strategy based on three fronts. They are:

  1. Menu innovation;
  2. Increased paid advertising; and
  3. Focus on Dutch Rewards.

Of course, we can consider the mobile ordering channel as one of these initiatives.

For menu innovation, Dutch Bros brought back fan-favorites Hazelnut Truffle Mocha and Candy Cane Mocha for the holiday season. They also added a new drink, Winter Shimmer Rebel. And in early 2025, for Valentine’s Day, they’re launching the Dutch Luv duo, Chocolate Covered Strawberry Mocha and Strawberries & Cream Rebel.

Note that this mix of seasonal items, especially established LTOs, creates tailwinds for same-store sales from increased transaction volume.

Speaking of paid advertising, Dutch Bros is using it not only in late-penetration markets, but also in established markets. Management said that during the second half of 2024 brand awareness in mature markets has increased even further!

Now, talking about Dutch Rewards, which accounts for about 71% of the company’s total sales in FY 2024, or $909.5 million (last year it accounted for 66% of Dutch Bros’ total sales, or approximately $637.4 million), it really was a success! Note that this number has already surpassed Starbucks Rewards' numbers, which had 41% of its sales within the United States indexed to app members during FY 2024.

Rewards members made approximately 5.5 million transactions through mobile ordering throughout FY 2024! Keep in mind that this only started in mid-2024, which just shows the potential of these two platforms together.

But there is one more potential traffic lever that I already warned you about during my last article: Food!

Management said early results are encouraging, with food still accounting for 2% of total Q4 sales (about $6.9 million). In fact, they also said they are losing morning beverage transactions from potential customers who are not satisfied with our current food offerings. Now, the initial test has very few items, so they are generating incremental dollars from guests who visit the drive-thru to purchase beverages.

It turns out that as they expand the offerings, selecting a mix of desirable foods, they will be able to generate incremental beverage sales from guests who are looking for food, especially during breakfast. Keep in mind that this is a guest who is still over-indexed on traditional fast food, especially Wendy's (WEN), and is looking for a cheap meal before work. Once they get that, they will be on to a gold mine.

Keeping an Eye on Finances

As we’ve seen, top-line sales increased 34.9% year-over-year during Q4 and 32.6% year-over-year for FY 2024. This marks Dutch Bros moving into a company with over $1 billion in annual sales!

Looking at cost centers, Food, Beverage and Packaging accounted for 25.4% of total revenue in the fourth quarter (about $87.1 million). They improved 120 points year-over-year, taking Dutch Bros to at least 460 percentage points below the '30/30/30 Rule' benchmark.

Something threatens the integrity of this result for FY 2025. The price of coffee has soared more than 119% over the past year! As of the day this article is being written, the rally appears to have lost steam, but it is still trading above $4.10. If this scenario persists, Dutch Bros expects COGS to fall 110 basis points over FY 2025.

That is, if the price of coffee remains above the $4 level for an extended period of time, Food, Beverage and Packaging costs will be approximately 26.5% of revenue (approximately $417 million for the entire FY 2025), reversing part of the advances the company achieved during FY 2024. Note that although this impacts the P&L, it is not something disastrous as the bears sell.

Labor Costs and Overheads remain in the range of 27.1% and 17.5% of total revenue respectively.

These improvements have benefited margins. See how they have behaved over the past few years in the following chart:

Note that all margins saw benefits from sales leverage, successful SG&A leverage (which reduced its proportionality to revenue by at least 400 basis points year-on-year), reduction in debt costs and cost containment.

This is especially important as Dutch Bros is increasingly becoming a self-funding company. Note that during FY 2024, the company generated a record OCF of $246.4 million, exceeding Capex and generating FCF for the first time since it transitioned from a coffee shop chain expanding through franchising to company-operated stores! This is quite unusual when we look at growth stocks. They often sacrifice profitability in order to grow, but that doesn't seem to be the case here.

By generating FCF and not paying dividends, Dutch Bros no longer needs to issue as much short-term debt as it used to. Remember that this was one of the central themes of my first article about the company? How time flies!

Dutch Bros secured long-term financing at the beginning of the year, which allows them to maintain a very satisfactory level of liquidity. They have both a positive NWC of $154 million and $293.4 million in cash on hand. This is a very cautious position to deal with a possible increase in operating asset accounts, which gives them great flexibility to negotiate terms without depending on their operating liabilities.

You could argue that this level of liquidity is unnecessary, and I would agree. But we can’t afford to play around with the Scissors Effect, especially in a company that is growing as fast as Dutch Bros At the end of the day, they only have $217 million in long-term debt, which implies a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 0.99x, which is almost risk-free. Add in the cash on hand and you’ll see that analyzing their debt sustainability is pretty much irrelevant here.

Rome wasn't built in a day. Understanding how Dutch Bros changed its expansion model for corporate stores and the impacts of this on its cash flow is very interesting. Note that in 2021, when the company started investing more in opening its drive-thrus and went public, the OCF generation was not enough to meet such a high investment need. Time passed and here we are. FY 2024 will be remembered as the year in which Dutch Bros became a self-financing company!

Looking ahead, management released the following guidance for FY 2025:

  • Total revenues are projected to be between $1.555 billion and $1.575 billion. This represents approximately 21% to 23% year-over-year growth;
  • Open at least 160 new stores, representing a system growth of 16%;
  • Same-store sales growth for the system is estimated at 2% to 4% for the full year;
  • Capital expenditures are estimated to be in the range of $240 million to $260 million, comprised primarily of new store construction costs;
  • Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $265 million and $275 million, representing 15% to 20% year-over-year growth.

Valuation: How to Price This Growth?

It's both interesting and funny how many analysts try to value fast-growing companies. Don't get me wrong, I include myself here. It's a daunting task!

Nights change, tables turn, and many growth stocks are being looked at less fondly than they once were. Wingstop, Sweetgreen, and CAVA (CAVA) are classic examples. But Dutch Bros has something different. It really could be America's next favorite.

When comparing it to other coffee chains like Starbucks (SBUX), we can't use the same yardstick. Dutch Bros is a fast-growing stock, so it's better to compare it to trendy fast-casuals. That way we can see how much the market would be willing to pay for that growth.

I've selected almost all of the hottest fast-casual restaurants in the world right now, including Shake Shack (SHAK), Sweetgreen, CAVA, Wingstop, and Portillo's.

Our mindset here is to define the size of the premium that investors are willing to pay for the growth of these restaurants and try to find a target price for Dutch Bros based on that. Now, if they're paying that premium for those stocks, why not for this coffee shop that's rocking it?

Just one more caveat. I used sales multiples, as they are the most appropriate for valuing fast-growing companies. If we were to use P/E or multiples such as EV/EBITDA or EV/EBIT, we could bias the valuation since these companies are at different stages of their growth. An example is Sweetgreen, which does not yet generate net income, or even Portillo's, which already generates strong operating cash flow, as well as Dutch Bros There are many facets that we need to look at here, it is easier to use a more general approach.

The result is interesting, and may tell us that there is more upside to come. Take a look at this:

  1. The average P/S of comparable companies is 7.87. Considering top-line sales of $1.555 billion, in line with the lower guidance estimates for FY 2025, we have an RPS of $11.92. This would give us a PT of $93.83. Now using top-line sales of $1.575 billion, we arrive at an RPS of $12.07, which implies a PT of approximately $95.02. The average of these two RPS estimates gives us a PT of$94.42;
  2. The average EV/S of comparable companies is 8.89. Considering top-line sales of $1.555 billion and the company's current capital structure, we have a PT of $100.94. Similarly, when we use top-line sales of $1.575, we have a PT of $102.30. Averaging the two gives us a PT of$101.62.

Note that this gives us a PT of around$98-$100 based on revenue estimates for Dutch Bros in FY 2025. They may beat those estimates later on, but it seems that based on the valuation of other restaurant chains out there, Dutch Bros could still have at least 17% upside.

So in my opinion, the rally is not likely to stop now. I reiterate my 'Buy' rating here, very bullish on this coffee chain that is getting everything right that Starbucks is getting wrong.

data.title: Allspring Emerging Markets Equity Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The MSCI EM Index (Net) declined 8.01% in the quarter as the index fell sharply in October and November.

U.S. elections, uncertain trade policy, the strong dollar, China’s struggling economy, and domestic political challenges in emerging markets were market drivers. In October, the U.S. election weighed on risk assets, Indian equities fell as growth slowed, and South Africa performed strongly. In November, Trump’s electoral victory raised the specter of protectionist trade policies and greater geopolitical tension, and China and exporters lagged. In December, emerging markets outperformed the S&P 500 Index and the MSCI EAFE Index (Net), led by Taiwan technology stocks, Gulf countries that benefited from their currency pegs to the U.S. dollar, and China, while Korea dealt with a political crisis.

Emerging market currencies fell 3.59% versus the U.S. dollar, as the U.S. Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) “hawkish cut” and a surging U.S. dollar took the wind out of the sails of rate-sensitive countries. Twenty-one of 24 emerging market currencies fell, and the currencies of Hungary and Korea declined the most.

Performance in the MSCI EM Index was broadly weak as 10 of 11 sectors and 20 of 24 countries fell. The largest outperformers included information technology, financials, UAE, Taiwan, and Kuwait, while laggards included materials, consumer discretionary, Brazil, Korea, and Indonesia.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

2025 is shaping up to be a volatile year for assets globally, but we continue to find attractive opportunities in emerging markets at the country, sector, and company level. The incoming U.S. government has proposed tariffs of 10% to 60% on some emerging market countries, setting the stage for a global trade war. However, some tariff proposals may be intended for use as bargaining chips to gain concessions on other issues. Further, emerging economies have been adjusting to tariff threats for several years. It seems likely to us that new tariffs may have less impact than expected. The U.S. dollar has strengthened since the November election, but the greenback has become extremely expensive by many measures. Should the U.S. dollar slow its ascent, it would be a positive surprise for emerging market assets. We believe that emerging market companies are in better shape than markets appear to acknowledge and that companies are optimistic about the future. In fact, Purchasing Managers’ Index data in most emerging market countries looks much healthier than in the U.S. or Europe. Finally, the valuation discount between emerging and developing markets has continued to widen, and valuations in the developed world are stretched. We think 2025 may be more positive for emerging market equities than investors expect.

data.title: Higher Fee Revenues, Brighter Outlook Boost Mood Of Private Equity's Big 4
data.description:
Record revenues from management fees and a brighter outlook for M&A in the year ahead helped private equity's Big Four close 2024 on a high note.

The average net positivity score for the four largest listed alternative asset managers - Apollo Global Management Inc. (APO), Blackstone Inc. (BX), The Carlyle Group Inc. (CG) and KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) - rose to its highest level in several years, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of the language used by executives and analysts on fourth-quarter 2024 earnings calls.

Strong fee-related earnings (FRE), a key measure of asset manager performance and profitability, gave executives something to crow about. Carlyle and KKR set annual records for FRE in 2024, while both Apollo and Blackstone reported their best-ever quarterly FRE marks in the fourth quarter.

The executives' 2025 outlook also leaned positive, with many predicting higher M&A levels as cheaper financing and pent-up demand drive transaction activity. Blackstone CFO Michael Chae said this should include a healthy dose of private equity exits, whose scarcity in recent years has hindered fund managers' ability to raise money from investors.

"We see a much more constructive environment for realizations in 2025," Chae said.

AUM update

The Big Four firms all reported higher FRE in 2024 compared to 2023. KKR's FRE grew the most year over year, increasing 37% to $3.27 billion, or $3.66 per share, in 2024.

KKR also ran just behind Apollo on two key growth metrics. The firm increased total assets under management 15.3% year-over-year to $637.53 billion by the end of the fourth quarter, and raised the portion of AUM that generates management fees 14.7% to $512 billion over the same period. Apollo's year-end 2024 totals of $751.04 billion in AUM and $568.7 billion in fee-earning AUM were both up 15.4% from the fourth quarter of 2023.

Meanwhile, Carlyle lagged its peers on both metrics. The firm grew AUM just 3.5% year-over-year in 2024 while the portion of AUM that generates fees shrank nearly 1% over the same period.

Carlyle CFO John Redett put a positive spin on the decline in the firm's fee-earning AUM, noting that exits from private equity investments factored into the drop.

"Realizations are good in our business in the sense we're giving capital back to our [limited partners (LPs)]. Our LPs like to see capital return," Redett said.

Credit drives fundraising

Private credit strategies continue to be a top priority for the Big Four's LPs and are the main driver of their fundraising.

Blackstone, the largest of the four by AUM, also led its peer group in fundraising, reporting a $171.5 billion haul in 2024. Of that total, 53%, or $91.2 billion, flowed to the firm's private credit strategies, while Blackstone's various private equity strategies attracted $41.3 billion, or about 24% of all capital committed to the firm in 2024.

Fundraising was even more lopsided at Apollo. The firm's private credit strategies drew $142.6 billion in new commitments in 2024, accounting for nearly 94% of the $152.4 billion raised by the firm in 2023.

"Credit is simply more attractive in absolute terms in the environment we're in," Apollo CEO Marc Rowan said.

Total return performance

Apollo's stock turned in the best performance between the start of 2024 and the end of the fourth-quarter 2024 earnings season in mid-February 2025, delivering a 71.5% total return to investors over that period, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. KKR followed with a total return of 69.5%.

Total returns of 27.1% for Blackstone and 26.7% for Carlyle slightly lagged the S&P 500, which produced total returns of 28.8% over that period.

Dividend forecast

Blackstone's dividend of $1.44 per share was the highest among the four firms in the fourth quarter. That dividend is expected to fall in 2025, according to Eclipse, an S&P Global dividend forecasting service.

Recently updated forecasts for Apollo and KKR predict both will continue with annual dividend increases, citing the firms' reported growth in fee revenue.
